title: Monetary Policy in a Shifting Landscape

paragraphs.text: Governor Centeno, fellow central bankers, distinguished guests. Thank you for joining us here in Sintra for the ECB Forum on Central Banking. This year’s theme, "Macroeconomic Stabilisation in a Volatile Inflation Environment," could not be more timely. The inflation landscape has shifted fundamentally, and our response must be calibrated to these new realities.

Let me begin with a clear statement of where we stand. Headline inflation in the euro area has come down significantly from its peak of over 10 percent in October 2022. This decline has been driven primarily by falling energy prices and the easing of supply chain disruptions. But we should not mistake the direction for the destination. Inflation remains too high and is projected to remain above our target for an extended period.

Core inflation — which strips out volatile energy and food prices — has proven more persistent than many anticipated. It remained at 5.3 percent in May, and the underlying dynamics suggest that the disinflation process in core components will be gradual and uneven. Services inflation, in particular, has shown considerable stickiness, reflecting the lagged effects of energy costs passing through to service sector pricing and the tightness of labor markets across the euro area.

The labor market in the euro area remains remarkably resilient. Unemployment stands at a historic low of 6.5 percent. Employment has grown by 3.5 million since the end of the pandemic. Nominal wage growth has picked up substantially, which is to be expected and indeed welcome after a period during which workers experienced a significant decline in real purchasing power. But we must monitor the interplay between wages, profits, and prices very carefully.

There is an important question about the role of profit margins in the current inflation dynamics. Our analysis suggests that in several sectors, firms have expanded their profit margins as they passed on cost increases — and in some cases more than cost increases — to consumers. If margins begin to compress, as input costs moderate, this would facilitate the disinflation process. If they do not, it would indicate a more structural shift in pricing behavior that monetary policy would need to address.

The Governing Council has raised interest rates by a cumulative 400 basis points since July 2022, bringing the deposit facility rate to 3.5 percent. This is the fastest and most significant tightening cycle in the history of the ECB. The transmission of our monetary policy decisions to financing conditions has been strong and rapid. Bank lending rates have risen sharply, and credit growth to both households and firms has decelerated markedly.

However, the transmission of tighter financing conditions to the real economy and ultimately to inflation operates with significant and variable lags. We are still in the early stages of seeing the full effects of our policy actions on economic activity and price developments. This uncertainty about the timing and magnitude of transmission requires us to be both determined and patient. We must stay the course for as long as necessary, but we must also remain attentive to the data as it unfolds.

The macroeconomic outlook for the euro area is characterized by considerable uncertainty. Growth has been weak, with GDP essentially stagnating in recent quarters. The manufacturing sector, which is more interest-rate-sensitive, has contracted. The services sector has been more resilient but is showing signs of deceleration. The balance of risks to the growth outlook is tilted to the downside, while risks to the inflation outlook remain tilted to the upside.

Let me now turn to a more structural question that I believe will define the conduct of monetary policy in the years ahead. We may be entering a period in which the macroeconomic environment is fundamentally different from the pre-pandemic era. The disinflationary forces that prevailed for a decade — globalization, cheap energy, abundant labor supply — may be giving way to a new regime characterized by greater supply-side volatility and more persistent inflationary pressures.

Geopolitical fragmentation is reshaping global supply chains. The transition to net zero will require massive investment but will also involve transition costs and potential energy price volatility. Demographic shifts, including aging populations, may reduce labor supply and put upward pressure on wages. If these structural changes materialize, central banks may face a more challenging environment in which supply shocks are more frequent and the trade-off between price stability and output stabilization is more acute.

In this context, the anchoring of inflation expectations becomes even more critical. I am encouraged that long-term inflation expectations in the euro area have remained well anchored around our 2 percent target throughout this inflationary episode. This reflects the credibility that the ECB has built over more than two decades, and it is a credibility that we must preserve at all costs. If expectations were to become de-anchored, the cost of restoring price stability would be far greater.

Our monetary policy decisions will continue to be guided by three criteria: the inflation outlook, the dynamics of underlying inflation, and the strength of monetary policy transmission. We are not committed to a predetermined rate path. Each decision will be taken meeting by meeting, based on the totality of incoming data. But let me be unambiguous: we will do whatever is necessary to bring inflation back to our 2 percent medium-term target in a timely manner.

The quantitative tightening process is also proceeding in an orderly fashion. The reduction of our balance sheet through the gradual run-off of the Asset Purchase Programme portfolio is progressing without significant market disruption. The reinvestment flexibility embedded in our pandemic portfolio allows us to manage any fragmentation risks that may arise. We are normalizing our balance sheet while preserving the effectiveness of our policy framework.

I want to reflect briefly on the fiscal dimension. Monetary policy cannot and should not bear the entire burden of macroeconomic stabilization. Fiscal policy must play its part. In the current environment, fiscal policy should be oriented toward reducing high debt levels and building buffers for future shocks. Untargeted fiscal measures that boost demand would work at cross-purposes with monetary policy and ultimately prolong the period of tight financial conditions.

At the same time, there is a clear need for targeted investment in areas that enhance the productive capacity of the European economy. The green and digital transitions require substantial public and private investment. The European Commission’s NextGenerationEU program provides a framework for such investment, but implementation must be accelerated. Structural reforms that improve competition, enhance labor market flexibility, and promote innovation are essential complements to sound macroeconomic policies.

Central banking has always required judgment under uncertainty. But the current juncture demands an even higher degree of analytical humility and intellectual openness. The models and frameworks that served us well in the pre-pandemic era may not fully capture the dynamics of the world we are entering. We must be willing to update our understanding, to challenge our assumptions, and to adapt our toolkit as circumstances evolve.

I am confident in the ECB’s capacity to fulfil its mandate. We have the tools, the expertise, and the determination to deliver price stability for the people of the euro area. Inflation will return to our target. The question is not whether, but when. And our task is to ensure that the path back to 2 percent is achieved in a timely manner, without undue cost to the economy. Thank you for your attention, and I look forward to our discussions over the coming days.
